author: @JessWLStuart
text: How about this: use a coolant on the back of a solar panel to keep it cool.  Take the coolant, which is heated by the back of the solar panel, to heat the hot part of a Stirling engine or engines.  Reclaim the waste heat!

replies.author: @bearnaff9387
replies.text:
You're talking about a very small amount of relative temperature difference when it comes to Stirling Engines.   Stirling engines require a working fluid to expand and contract in order to create motion from heat.  Air is the working fluid in most engines and it really only gets efficient at around 160C.  A more compressible working fluid will be efficient at lower temperature differences, but the go-to in that case is Helium, which is getting pricey to procure and imposes its own wear and tear on things trying to contain it.

All that said, you totally could use some kind of thermal energy harvesting system to generate a little more power per sq. meter of solar array.  It really depends on whether you need the extra power badly enough.  It's close enough to impossible that I'm OK with claiming that it's impossible for such a system to generate enough electricity to exceed the cost of making the engine and hookups in the first place.  If you must use solar as your power input and space is the limiting factor instead of material costs or weight, it definitely makes sense to Stirling up.  I just can't think of scenario where that's true.
